commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: Human review is needed for the sanitizer privacy/content-loss tradeoff, and the external contributor must add real behavior proof before merge.; Needs attention: Needs attention because the PR changes a privacy-sensitive outbound sanitizer and still has broad, unproven content-deletion behavior.; Review finding: [P1] Narrow first-person preamble triggers — src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.ts:770-771 - [P1] Gat... (sha=c2177fcaf358e92b02a229abd817e65aa6caae52)
commands.review_summary:
The branch adds delivery-profile stripping for leaked reasoning preambles and structural contamination, wires it into user-facing reply sanitization, and adds focused sanitizer tests while also carrying unrelated formatting churn across plugins, scripts, tests, and UI files.

Reproducibility: yes. for the PR blockers by source inspection: passing normal two-line first-person prose or a fenced JSON schema example through the new delivery sanitizer deterministically hits the stripping logic. I did not run live Telegram/Gemini proof in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: Human review is needed for the sanitizer privacy/content-loss tradeoff, and the external contributor must add real behavior proof before merge.

target.title: Strip leaked reasoning preambles before outbound send
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: some providers can leak plain-text reasoning/planning preambles into the final outbound reply even when tagged reasoning stripping already exists.
- Why it matters: users can receive internal-looking assistant text instead of only the intended visible answer.
- What changed: added a last-mile outbound cleaner in reply normalization, plus conservative preamble scoring that now ignores code regions and treats pure CJK as a weaker signal unless paired with other suspicious cues.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change provider prompting policy, transport behavior, model selection, or force `<final>`-style tagging.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #60551
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: outbound reply normalization relied on tagged scaffolding removal, but plain-text leaked reasoning preambles could still survive to channel delivery.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no final text sanitizer in `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)` for plain-text planning/reasoning lead-ins.
- Contributing context (if known): GLM/Kimi-style outputs can mix visible replies with untagged internal-looking preambles, and the first heuristic draft needed follow-up hardening to avoid pure-CJK and code-fence false positives.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - suspicious English planning preamble is stripped before visible reply text
  - mixed CJK + suspicious lead-in can still be stripped when signals agree
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK replies are preserved
  - suspicious-looking text inside fenced code blocks does not trigger stripping
  - reply normalization applies the cleaner before final send
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared text sanitation plus the reply normalization seam, so unit + seam coverage hits the failure line directly without needing full channel E2E.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): tagged scaffolding stripping already existed, but not the plain-text leaked-preamble cases added here.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Final outbound replies are more aggressively cleaned when a suspicious internal-looking preamble appears before a user-facing answer.
- Legitimate multi-paragraph replies are preserved more carefully after the follow-up heuristic hardening.
- Suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks no longer contributes to preamble scoring.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping only]
  -> [plain-text leaked preamble may survive]
  -> [user sees internal-looking lead-in]

After:
[assistant/model output]
  -> [tag stripping]
  -> [plain-text leaked-preamble cleaner]
  -> [reply normalization]
  -> [user sees only intended visible reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js / pnpm workspace checkout
- Model/provider: bug motivated by GLM/Kimi-style reply behavior; patch is provider-agnostic in the outbound sanitizer seam
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram/OpenClaw auto-reply path locally, plus shared outbound normalization path
- Relevant config (redacted): default local OpenClaw workspace config; no config schema changes

### Steps

1. Produce reply text that starts with suspicious planning/reasoning lines and then switches into a user-facing answer.
2. Pass that text through `stripAssistantInternalScaffolding(...)` and `normalizeReplyPayloadDirectives(...)`.
3. Compare behavior for normal multi-paragraph replies, CJK-only replies, and fenced-code-block cases.

### Expected

- Internal-looking preambles are removed when a clear visible answer follows.
- Legitimate replies without enough suspicious signal are preserved.
- Code-fenced suspicious text does not trigger stripping.

### Actual

- Updated implementation matches expected behavior in targeted runtime assertions and added tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - imported the changed modules successfully with `pnpm exec tsx`
  - ran explicit runtime assertions covering English leaked-preamble stripping, mixed CJK suspicious stripping, normal CJK preservation, fenced-code preservation, and reply-delivery integration
  - patched the local installed OpenClaw runtime and confirmed gateway health after restart
- Edge cases checked:
  - ordinary multi-paragraph English reply with mild meta phrasing
  - ordinary multi-paragraph CJK reply
  - suspicious-looking content inside fenced code blocks
  - reply text reduced to trimmed visible output before send
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repo-wide `pnpm check` completion in this environment; local pre-commit hook stalled/SIGKILLed inside the broader `tsgo` step, so this PR relies on targeted verification for the touched seam instead of claiming a full green repo validation

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: heuristic stripping could still remove an unusual but legitimate preamble-like reply.
  - Mitigation: scoring is conservative, code regions are excluded, pure CJK is no longer sufficient by itself to hit the threshold, and targeted tests lock in the main false-positive cases.
- Risk: provider-specific leak patterns may evolve beyond the current heuristics.
  - Mitigation: cleaner is isolated in shared text sanitation, making future pattern refinement small and local.
target.author: sweetsophia
